We recommended and the Management agreed that MTO make a further study of the features of the ETracs in order to ensure that the system is utilized to its full potential which would ultimately enhance the productivity and efficiency of the MTO’s operation resulting in the generation of relevant and reliable reports.

We recommended and the Management, in coordination with the concerned department heads, agreed to conduct a more extensive site investigation during the preparation of detailed engineering plans; and ensure that priority projects included in the Annual Investment Plan and the proposed budget are “implementation ready”.
We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to release RPT shares to the barangays within five (5) days after the end of each quarter.

We recommended and Management agreed to send demand letters to the concerned municipal officers and employees to enforce immediate settlement of the outstanding cash advances and make a proper accounting or liquidation thereof.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that full liquidation of CAs is rendered first by the accountable officers before new ones are granted for stronger internal control providing safeguards on the disbursement of Municipal funds.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer, identify and submit the specific sources of funds used for the total investments in time deposits.
Additionally, we recommended and the Management agreed to ensure compliance with COA Circular No. 92-382 in the accurate computation of idle funds.

We recommended and Management agreed that the concerned employee receiving cash advances for the registration of motor vehicles be properly bonded with the Bureau of the Treasury in order to ensure proper coverage and guarantee indemnification in case of loss of funds.

We recommended and the Management agreed to conduct a thorough evaluation of the current salary structure of LGU officials and employees before entering into any Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with external financial institutions for salary deduction of loan amortizations. This assessment should ensure that the employees' net take-home pay does not fall below the legally required minimum, pursuant to Section 56 of the GAA.
We recommended and the MSWDO agreed to justify in writing and submit the necessary documents to substantiate the payments for hazard pay in CY 2024.

We recommended that Management discontinue paying for the procurement of supplies, materials and meals through reimbursement and instead issue checks directly to the service provider, ensuring that the withholding tax is properly computed and deducted in accordance with RR 14-2002.
The management explained that that there are inevitable instances like emergencies where they would process reimbursements. Moreover, before processing reimbursements they would require justification. Also, they proposed that they would revisit their reimbursement internal policies.

We recommended and the Management agreed to submit a written explanation addressing the noted observation and take immediate steps to facilitate a formal name change to reflect the correct entity name and ensure that government funds shall be used solely for public purposes.

We recommended and the Management agreed to provide a written explanation addressing the noted observation.
Additionally, we recommended and the Management agreed to take immediate action to demand the recovery of the unrecouped mobilization fee from the contractor.
Henceforth, we strongly recommended and Management agreed strict compliance with Section 5.3 of Annex E of RA 9184 to ensure proper recoupment of advance payments in future projects.
The Management explained that the mobilization for certain projects, Items 3 and 4, could not be deducted because there has been no final billing at that time. On the other hand, Items 1 and 2, is still open due to the failure of the contractor to process their final billing. Moreover, the Management has initiated coordination with the Accounting and Engineering offices to strengthen monitoring and enforcement of full mobilization recoupment during billing processes.

We recommended and Management agreed that, in coordination with all Department Heads responsible for supervising and monitoring specific programs, projects, and activities (PPAs), ensure the prompt implementation of any remaining unexecuted PPAs and submit complete fund utilization reports in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013.
Additionally, for PPAs that have already been implemented but remain unliquidated, we recommended and Management agreed to expedite the submission of liquidation reports to clear outstanding balances in liability accounts and enhance the accuracy and reliability of financial statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to provide an explanation for the payment of overtime compensation to JO workers despite the absence of a corresponding provision in their contracts.
We further recommended and Management agreed to direct the HRMO to evaluate the work assignments of JO workers and revise their contracts accordingly clearly defining their expected duties, including any provisions related to working hours and additional services rendered beyond the standard schedule.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to recognize withholding tax upon recording the liability at year-end by crediting Due to BIR and recording Accounts Payable at net of tax to minimize the risk of administrative penalties and ensure the accurate presentation of financial statements.

Further, management will refrain from turning over accountabilities by one DO to another without fully liquidating the same pursuant to Paragraph 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 in order to properly establish accountability. 

We also recommended and Management agreed to refrain from granting new CAs to the DO when previous CAs are not yet fully liquidated.

We recommended and Management agreed to require (a) the Municipal Treasurer to make the necessary steps to cancel all stale checks, present them in the RCI, attach to the original DVs and supporting documents, and return to the Municipal Accountant; and (b) the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary accounting entries upon receipt of the required documents from the Municipal Treasurer to bring the affected accounts to their correct balances, in conformity with Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs Volume I.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to recompute the depreciation of the components of road network system without providing 5% residual value and make the necessary entries to correct the balances of affected accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit explanation for the delay in the implementation of target priority development projects for the 20% DF.

Henceforth, management will fast track and prioritize the implementation of the remaining CY 2022 projects; and ensure that target projects for the 20% DF for the succeeding years are fully implemented.

Prospectively, strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2014-001 on the submission of the annual GAD Accomplishment Report will be enforced.

We recommended that management see to it that acquisition of land has to be supported with complete documentary requirements as required in Item 13.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to substantiate the propriety and validity of the payment as well as to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended that management demand the liquidation of the financial assistance granted to LGUs upon expected completion of the project or the intended purpose has been served in accordance with existing regulations and to refrain from extending grant to recipients’ unliquidated financial assistance.

observations.recommendation:
Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the GSO and Inventory Committee to consider in the succeeding PPE
counts a detailed verification and identification of the Municipality’s properties
and adopt COA Circular No. 2020-006;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation with GSO and
review the noted discrepancies between their records, make the necessary
adjustments and provide disclosures in the notes to financial statements for
variances which cannot be adjusted; and
c. create a Disposal and Appraisal Committee that will (i) inspect unserviceable
equipment and property; (ii) set the final appraised value of all disposable
properties; (iii) recommend the disposal to the Local Chief Executive for his
approval and the manner of disposal; and (iv) forward the approved documents
to the Municipal Accountant for the necessary adjustments in the PPE.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. make the necessary entries for prior year adjustments for the understatement
of assets totaling P24,885,407.68 and for any related accumulated depreciation
for the unrecorded PPEs;
b. recognize income and expenses in the Trust Fund books upon utilization of
funds received from national agencies or any other sources, whether private or
government, that are considered as non-exchange transactions and record
entries relevant to the transactions; and
c. prepare and submit for succeeding years the Statement of Financial
Performance for the Trust Fund along with the other financial statements of the
Municipality.

Thus, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to consistently prepare and submit to the Audit Team
bank reconciliation statements of all Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality;
b. Municipal Accountant to verify the accuracy of the negative cash in bank
balance pertaining to market operations of the Municipality and prepare the
necessary correction entries, if any; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to prepare the list of unreleased checks every year-end in
time for the closing of the books and submit to the Municipal Accountant and
the latter to restore to the Cash in Bank and proper liability accounts the amount
corresponding to the unreleased checks at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant, in
cooperation with the Budget Officer, to reconcile the figures in SCBAA and in the
Statement of Financial Performance, have it disclosed in the Notes to Financial
Statements as required in IPSAS 24 and ensure its prompt submission to the
Commission on Audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
draw, if still necessary, journal entry voucher to correct the entries in recording
advance payment made to the contractor. Subsequently, ensure that proper entries
will be taken up in the books for future transactions involving such advance
payments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. direct the Municipal Engineer and/or MPDC to submit justification on the noted
observation and submit immediately all lacking documents to the Office of the
Auditor for verification and audit;
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of the supporting
documents as required under Section 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 before
processing payments to suppliers and contractors; Henceforth, comply strictly with
Section 9.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and
c. Lastly, Instruct the Municipal Accountant to make adjusting entries to
reclassify the completed projects.

We recommended that the Mayor require all concerned Department
Heads/employees to submit detailed action plans on the CY 2020 AAR findings and
recommendations, including that of prior years, to be incorporated in the AAPSI,
and to submit the AAPSI to the Audit Team. Likewise, assign a focal person who
will prepare the AAPSI and monitor the compliance of each concerned Department
Head/employee regarding the submission of detailed action plans.

Moreover, we recommended that the management follow strictly the above
provisions of the GAA and COA Memorandum No. 2014-002 on the submission of
the AAPSI within 60 days from the receipt of the COA Annual Audit report to give
enough time for the Auditor to review, monitor and evaluate the action taken by the
management on the audit recommendations.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned personnel
to indicate in the PO the complete specifications of the items being requisitioned,
including brand names and all other necessary information relative to the
procurement of supplies/goods/property, to facilitate the inspection and the
determination of the reasonableness of the prices and quality of the items procured
and delivered.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRM Officer to
comply with the required annual preparation and updating of DRRMFIP for any
changes as to initial disaster management plans and for allocation of unexpended
previous year’s DRRM balance accruing to the special trust fund of the Municipality.

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to make the necessary journal entry to reclassify and present the correct year-end
balances of the affected accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepares and submits the Monthly
Report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF to the Audit Team to facilitate
the review and monitoring of the sources and utilization of the fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
verify the accuracy of the figures presented in the Statement of Cash Flows
particularly any misclassification of cash flow activities and observe the disclosure
requirements in the preparation of the financial statements in accordance with
IPSAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Service Officer to conduct a thorough annual physical inventory count of the PPEs, identify all the items included in the lump sum amount and provide the required details of the latter. We also recommended the verification of the assets’ ownership relevant to their existence and completeness of records in the accounting books prior to making the necessary entry or subsequent derecognition. Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation of accounts and the GSO to facilitate the review of the noted variances between their records, effect the necessary adjustments to arrive at the reconciled balance and incorporate the necessary disclosures in the notes to financial statements.

observations.reason: There are PPEs in the report of physical count which are lacking complete description hence are not specifically identified. Likewise, location of the PPEs and its actual condition are also not provided in the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Service Officer to conduct a thorough annual physical inventory count of the PPEs, identify all the items included in the lump sum amount and provide the required details of the latter. We also recommended the verification of the assets’ ownership relevant to their existence and completeness of records in the accounting books prior to making the necessary entry or subsequent derecognition. Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation of accounts and the GSO to facilitate the review of the noted variances between their records, effect the necessary adjustments to arrive at the reconciled balance and incorporate the necessary disclosures in the notes to financial statements.
observations.managementAction: There was a conducted reconciliation showing that the variance between the GSO and Accounting books.
observations.reason: The variance of P124,446,056.84 remains unaccounted. Necessary accounting entries are also not yet taken up in the accounting books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. make the necessary entries for prior year adjustments for the understatement of income and government equity totaling P7,055,579.71, understatement of PPE by P2,048,060.21 and understatement of expenses by P5,907,519.50; b. recognize income in the Trust Fund books upon utilization of funds received from national agencies or any other sources, whether private or government, that are considered as non-exchange transactions and record entries relevant to the transactions; and c. prepare and submit for succeeding years the Statement of Financial Performance for the Trust Fund along with the other financial statements of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Correcting entries were recorded in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct: a. the Municipal Treasurer to a.1) prepare and submit to the Municipal Accounting Office the Schedule of Unreleased Commercial Checks in time for the closing of the books to enable the reversion to cash in bank and payable accounts of the amount of unreleased checks; and a.2) for the checks which have become stale, send written notice to the payees for the existence of checks and require submission to facilitate issuance of new checks; b. the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) for the adjustments of the deficiencies noted herein and effect the necessary adjustments of reconciling items determined during the preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on a timely basis for correct and reliable Cash in Bank account balance in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Notices were sent to payees and new checks were issued. The schedule of unreleased checks was not forwarded to the Municipal Accountant.
observations.reason: Unreleased and stale checks are still not being adjusted to reflect accurate cash in bank balance at year end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to explain/justify the discrepancies noted between the prices of the medical supplies procured by the Municipality and their suggested retail prices during those times, and require the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking support to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Bids and Awards Committee and the head of the BAC Secretariat provided a justification that there were limited suppliers offering needed medical supplies and the urgency to acquire such led them to avail the most accessible option. The Municipal Mayor and head of the BAC Secretariat submitted the lacking documents to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDRRMO to submit: a. copy of the revised LDRRM Investment Plan including that for the Trust Liability – DRRM Fund; b. required reports for the receipt and distribution of donations in-kind to the Taal evacuees, otherwise submit justification on non-preparation and non-submission of the said reports.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO complied. A copy of the LDRRMF Investment Plan for CY 2020 including that for the Trust Liability – DRRM Fund was submitted. For CY 2022, MOOE for disaster has been budgeted separately into the office of the MDRRM. There is no report on donations in-kind since according to the MDRRMO, the donors directly distributed such to the evacuees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Local Chief Executive to coordinate with the PSOs in addressing the identified causes of delays and ensure compliance with the IMA, procurement law, I-Build Operations Manual and other pertinent rules and regulations in the subproject implementation. We also recommended the imposition of liquidated damages on the contractor for the delays.
observations.managementAction: Liquidated damages had been imposed on the contractor upon final payment. The project is 100% completed and accepted by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to provide justification on the delay of actual submission of liquidation documents and on the result of the validation of the beneficiaries showing that they were able to perform their functions accordingly.
observations.managementAction: The delay in submission of hard copies was caused by the conditions brought by the pandemic given the restrictions. Although an advanced liquidation was made via email on May 4, 2020 with complete supporting scanned documents explains that many of the beneficiaries were able to perform their functions accordingly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly implement the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 97-002 for the grant and liquidation of cash advance, particularly with respect to the prescriptive period for the latter. Finally, the Municipal Accountant must demand the submission of liquidation reports relative to the P472,890.00 cash advances and submit it to the Audit Team for verification. Otherwise, they should be considered unliquidated and the Municipal Accountant must withhold the salary of the concerned employees and officials.
observations.managementAction: Some of the liquidations were erroneously included on a different set of vouchers which pertains to petty cash reimbursements. Twenty-six vouchers were already submitted to the incumbent audit team. Other additional disbursement vouchers were submitted during the period of the incumbent audit team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to provide justification on the above-discussed audit observation. They should strictly implement the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 97-002 for the grant and liquidation of cash advance, particularly with respect to the prescriptive period for the latter. Finally, the Municipal Accountant must demand the submission of liquidation reports relative to the P2,390,253.03 cash advances. Otherwise, they should be considered unliquidated and the Municipal Accountant must withhold the salary of the concerned employees and officials.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted the liquidation reports. Other concerned offices settled.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant withhold those taxes arising from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date and payables & remitted following the month those expenses were recorded. The practice should be applied prospectively.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant withheld taxes from the vouchers for payment still recognized as payables and remitted the taxes following the month those expenses were recorded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the officials and employees concerned refrain from reimbursements and to process procurement and disbursement through disbursement basis and to comply strictly the relevant provisions of RA 9184 and BIR regulations on emergency purchases only. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the proper planning and consolidation for the procurement of the agency to minimize frequent but small purchases of supplies. Purchase of supplies are done bi-annually thru the procurement service of DBM.
observations.managementAction: The management agreed. Proper planning and consolidation will be implemented; purchases of supplies are done bi-annually through DBM procurement service.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Chief Executive to coordinate with the PSOs in addressing the identified causes of delays and ensure compliance with the IMA, procurement law, I-Build Operations Manual and other pertinent rules and regulations in the subproject implementation.
observations.managementAction: The project is 100% completed and accepted by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer establish the amount of real property taxes of those taxpayers with legal issues for proper disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements by the Municipal Accountant. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain ledger cards for the receivables of each taxpayer with amounts reconciled with the records of the Municipal Treasury and the Municipal Treasurer maintain the general ledger showing the summary.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer maintain the record per summary. The Municipal Accountant maintain ledger cards for the taxpayer receivable. Inventory of unused supplies at year-end was done and submitted to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Supply Officer to regularly maintain Stock Cards (SC) and Property Cards for supplies; create an Inventory Team to conduct physical inventory taking of all Inventory accounts each year and to submit the reconciled RPCI together with the SC to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation with the SLC in order to ascertain the correct inventory balances for a period.
observations.managementAction: Inventory of unused supplies at year-end was done and submitted to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider the full adoption of the guide in creating and operating the Local Economic Enterprises for the systematic review of operations and adoption of appropriate measures. Likewise, we recommended that the Honorable Members of the Sanggunian Bayan adopt an ordinance for the legal recognition of the said existing economic enterprises of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The LEE was formally established through ordinance passed by the Sangguniang Bayan. Separate books of accounts are being prepared for the LEE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to initiate through the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board the: a. establishment of Multi-Purpose Environment Cooperatives or Association to promote the public’s knowledge, interest and participation in achieving the goals of the Solid Waste Management Plans of the agency; b. creation and operation of materials recovery facilities and ensure compliance with the provisions for proper collection and transport of solid waste.
observations.managementAction: It is not cost-effective to establish a cooperative considering the very few willing participants or potential members. However efforts to protect public health and the environment continues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality establish the GAD Database to strengthen the planning and budgeting processes for GAD and ensure that planned PPAs address identified gender issues, that objectives, performance indicators and targets are well-formulated to facilitate monitoring and evaluation for the attainment of GAD objectives.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has an existing GAD Database.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant on the strict compliance of the accounting laws and rules and the immediate submission of the listed Disbursement Vouchers to the auditor to be subjected to audit and to prevent the unnecessary issuance of audit suspension and/or disallowance. Further, ensure that the accounts are kept in an organized and orderly manner and properly monitored for easy reference or for other reporting purposes.
observations.managementAction: Twenty-six vouchers were already submitted to the incumbent audit team. Other supporting disbursement vouchers were submitted during the period of the incumbent audit team. Liquidation reports and supporting documents were submitted to the Audit Team.
observations.reason: Still searching for the remaining unsubmitted disbursement vouchers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the officials and employees refrain from resorting to procurement and disbursement through reimbursement method and that the Municipality implement a consistent policy for the payment of consolidated communication expense, and that proper planning and consolidation for the procurement of the agency to minimize frequent but small purchases of supplies. Finally, that the Municipal Accountant require externally generated document to support the actual incurrence and correctness of the transaction and to mitigate the risk of manipulation and fabrication of the same.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has complied with the requirements of RA 9184 and BIR regulations on withholding of taxes. The Municipal Accountant informed that reimbursements are still being done for monthly communication expenses and vehicle rentals. Purchase of supplies are still being reimbursed. The Municipality provided justifications.
observations.reason: Reimbursements are still being made for monthly communication expenses and vehicle rentals. No satisfactory justification provided for non-compliance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer and the Inventory Committee to conduct inventory and inspection for unserviceable properties and embody in an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and facilitate their disposal of in accordance with the rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: No available personnel to do the task. GSO to prioritize the inventory and inspection for unserviceable properties and embody in an IIRUP.
observations.reason: Limited time and workforce allotted to do the task.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality regularly maintain Stock Cards (SC) and Property Cards for supplies; create an Inventory Team to conduct physical inventory taking of all Inventory accounts each year and to submit the reconciled RPCI together with the SLC in order to ascertain the correct inventory balances for a given period.
observations.managementAction: No available personnel to do the task. The Office will prioritize the conduct of physical inventory taking in the following periods.
observations.reason: Limited workforce and time to accomplish task; still searching for remaining documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Service Officer to
conduct a thorough annual physical inventory count of the PPEs, identify all the
items included in the lump sum amount and provide the required details of the
latter.

We also recommended the verification of the assets’ ownership relevant to their
existence and completeness of records in the accounting books prior to making the
necessary entry or subsequent derecognition. Disposal of the unserviceable
properties must also be taken into consideration to make room for the existing
serviceable properties in storage as well as to eliminate them from records.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to conduct reconciliation of accounts and the GSO to facilitate the
review of the noted variances between their records, effect the necessary
adjustments to arrive at the reconciled balance and incorporate the necessary
disclosures in the notes to financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:

a. make the necessary entries for prior year adjustments for the understatement of
income and government equity totaling ₱7,055,579.71, understatement of PPE
by ₱2,048,060.21 and understatement of expenses by ₱5,907,519.50;
b. recognize income in the Trust Fund books upon utilization of funds received
from national agencies or any other sources, whether private or government,
that are considered as non-exchange transactions and record entries relevant
to the transactions; and
c. prepare and submit for succeeding years the Statement of Financial
Performance for the Trust Fund along with the other financial statements of the
Municipality.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to draw adjusting journal entries to reduce the RPT and SET
Receivables for the amount of shares of other local government units, record
receivables and income for the delinquencies and recognize as income of prior
years the remaining balance of the liability accounts under the Deferred RPT and
SET Income under the Prior Period Adjustment account.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:

a. the Municipal Treasurer to a.1) prepare and submit to the Municipal
Accounting Office the Schedule of Unreleased Commercial Checks in time
for the closing of the books to enable the reversion to cash in bank and
payable accounts of the amount of unreleased checks; and a.2) for the
checks which have become stale, send written notice to the payees for the
existence of checks and require submission to facilitate issuance of new
checks;
b. the Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the Journal Entry
Vouchers (JEVs) for the adjustments of the deficiencies noted herein and
effect the necessary adjustments of reconciling items determined during the
preparation of the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements on a timely basis
for correct and reliable Cash in Bank account balance in the financial
statements.

However, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to explain/justify the discrepancies
noted between the prices of the medical supplies procured by the Municipality and
their suggested retail prices during the price freeze implementation.

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to submit the lacking support to the distribution of welfare goods as
detailed above.

For the noted deficiencies, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
MDRRMO to submit:

a. copy of the revised LDRRM Investment Plan including that for the Trust
Liability – DRRM Fund;
b. required reports for the receipt and distribution of donations in-kind to the
Taal evacuees, otherwise submit justification on non-preparation and non-
submission of the said reports

We also recommended that the MDRRMO and the Designated GSO to see to it that
subsequent in-kind donations are accounted and reported consistently as
prescribed in the aforementioned COA Circular.

Further, we reiterated that expenses pertaining to the regular repairs and insurance
of motor vehicles and disaster response equipment and all other disbursements
being administrative in nature be budgeted and charged to appropriate accounts
and ensure that all disbursements paid out of the DRRM Fund are in accordance
with the purpose it is intended.

Therefore, we recommended to require the imposition on the final payment to the
contractor of additional liquidated damages on the remaining 22 calendar days.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to provide
justification on the delay of actual submission of liquidation documents and on the
result of the validation of the beneficiaries showing some ineligibles.

We recommended that the GSO conduct a thorough annual physical inventory count of the PPE, taking into account only those actually counted and consider those items reported in lump sum if without particular details only for verification/clarification of the status of said PPE.
We also recommended the verification of the assets’ ownership relevant to their existence and completeness of records in the accounting books prior to making the necessary entry or subsequent derecognition. Disposal of the unserviceable properties must also be taken into consideration to make room for the existing serviceable properties in storage as well as to eliminate them from records.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to conduct reconciliation of accounts and the GSO to facilitate the review of the noted variances between their records, effect the necessary adjustments to arrive at the reconciled balance and incorporate the necessary disclosures in the notes to financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The management represented that they will comply with the audit recommendations and that the reconciliation of the PPE records of the Accounting Office and the GSO is ongoing as of date.
observations.reason: Some PPE items lack specific details and were still presented in lump sum amount due to unavailability of written records. The Inventory Committee headed by GSO, Mr. Demetrio B. Araja have just set a date for inspection of unserviceable PPE for disposal anyway. The Municipal Accounting Office provides summary of all beginning balances plus additions/transactions during the year. From there, GSO personnel will reconcile his physical count with the PPE balances recorded in books. Any discrepancy will be discussed in a meeting of the two offices to arrive at the correct balances as of that date. As for the ₱1,869,453.02 difference, it is composed of the administrative expenses that were capitalized, related and part of the PPE recorded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to strictly implement the guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 97-002 for the grant and liquidation of cash advance, particularly with respect to the prescriptive period for the latter. Finally, the Municipal Accountant must demand the submission of liquidation reports relative to the P2,390,253.03 cash advances. Otherwise, they should be considered unliquidated and the Municipal Accountant must withhold the salary of the concerned employees and officials.
observations.managementAction: The GSO stated that the liquidation reports transactions were already settled. They will comply.
observations.reason: Some cash advance transactions were settled despite absence of liquidation reports, and explanation/justification on the above-discussed audit observation is yet to be provided by management.

observations.recommendation: For the following year the Municipal Mayor requires the Municipal Treasurer to provide justification on the audit observation. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the MTO to collect taxes in accordance with the Municipality’s Revenue Code. The MTO should require the submission of sworn statement of gross receipts/sales or certified copies of income tax return so as to come-up with an accurate basis for the computation of business taxes, and to require the taxpayers to duly accomplish the application form prior to processing. Finally, the BPLO and the MTO should verify, assess and validate the noted undercollection of P25,307.30 and consequently demand the collection/payment thereof. The absence of documented action or information to counter the undercollection shall mean the issuance of the corresponding Notice of Charge.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer computes and collects taxes in accordance with the Municipality’s Revenue Code. The MTO will request the details of the taxpayers noted in audit for the details. For year 2020, upon discussion with concerned offices it was agreed that all taxpayers who will secure business permits will secure/submit a sworn statement of gross receipts/sales. The MTO will also require the submission of supporting documents. The MTO already collects taxes and issues receipts in accordance with the Municipality’s ordinances.

observations.recommendation: In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Management to withhold those taxes arising from the payables as from the date and apply the amounts withheld as payables & remitted the taxes. We also recommended that the Municipal Office of the Municipal Accountant to withhold taxes from the payables until remitted and to have a practice of treating the withholdings as payable until remittance.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Management stated that the office of the Municipal Treasurer recorded the expenses and the payments were already recorded and taxes withheld were already remitted following the month the transactions were paid.
observations.reason: Subsequently, taxes are still not being withheld for payables for reason that the Accounting Office thinks it is more practical to record and remit the tax withheld by the time the LGU pays the transactions; hence some taxes were recorded as paid.

observations.recommendation: Foregoing considered, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of the submission and completeness of the Driver’s Trip Ticket and that the fuel Consumption Report should be fully accomplished with complete details of the trip and gas refills. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the fuel consumption report to be submitted prior to her approval of the payment for the fuel consumption. The missing documents must be attached to the vouchers submitted.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant stated that the missing documents were actually attached to the vouchers submitted. It happened that the scanned copy of the transaction where those Sales Invoices are attached to the soft copy were not included in the submitted soft copy. It was settled already through the reply justification submitted by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Local Chief Executive to coordinate with the PSOs in addressing the identified causes of delays and ensure compliance with the IMA, procurement law, I-Build Operations Manual and other pertinent rules and regulations in the implementation. We further recommended that the Municipality impose liquidated damages as necessary for delays attributable to the contractor and require the contractor to expedite completion.
observations.managementAction: Management stated that the subproject is now 100% complete as to physical structure however due to the inability of the contractor to immediately comply to the recommended actions supplied by PRDP-DA and the LGU, the completion thereof cannot be declared 100% completed and accepted by the LGU. It was agreed to direct the contractor to expedite completion and they imposed liquidated damages.
observations.reason: The recommended actions were agreed but due to the inability of the contractor to comply, completion could not be declared 100% completed and accepted by the LGU until later. Delay mainly attributable to contractor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality impose liquidated damages, forfeit the contractor’s security and rescind the contract. We also recommended that the Municipality return to the BTr the unutilized ADM fund amounting to P3,596,712.98.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor formally terminated the contract in accordance with the procedures set forth in Annex I of 2016 Revised Rules of RA 9184. The unutilized amount of P3,596,712.98 was already refunded/returned to the Bureau of Treasury.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Planning and Development Office to submit the original and as-built plans; the Municipal Accountant to compute and claim the liquidated damages for the undelivered items and the Municipal Mayor to withhold final payment until full compliance.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC submitted the required document. The project had been completed and accepted. The contractor was already assessed and liquidated damages were imposed where applicable; the items were delivered and project was fully paid.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRMO to submit the revised LDRRM Investment Plan including that for the Trust Liability – DRRM Fund; required reports for the receipt and distribution of donations in-kind to the Taal evacuees, otherwise submit justification on non-preparation and non-submission of the said reports. We also recommended that the MDRRMO and the Designated GSO ensure subsequent in-kind donations are accounted and reported consistently as prescribed in COA Circulars. Further we reiterated that expenses pertaining to the regular repairs and insurance of motor vehicles and disaster response equipment and all other disbursements being administrative in nature be budgeted and charged to appropriate accounts and ensure that all disbursements paid out of the DRRM Fund are in accordance with the purpose it is intended.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO prepared the LDRRMF Investment Plan for CY 2020 and will furnish the Audit Team with a copy immediately. However, there is no report on donations in-kind since the donors directly distributed such to the evacuees. On charging repairs, maintenance and insurance of motor vehicles on the LDRRM Fund, the MDRRMO replied that he will request funding for such kind of expenses in order to avoid inappropriate charges to DRRM.
observations.reason: No Investment Plan copies were submitted at time of audit for some Trust Liability – DRRM charges; reports on donated in-kind goods were not prepared; some expenditures for repairs and insurance were charged to DRRM Fund though considered administrative in nature.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer establish the certified list of taxpayers tallied with the assessment report of the Municipal Assessor as of year-end; establish the amount of real property taxes of those taxpayers with legal issues for proper disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements; require the Municipal Accountant to maintain ledger cards for receivables of each taxpayer with reconciled records; and maintain Supplies Ledger Cards and related records for inventory monitoring.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer started with the encoding of the tallied data for the 26 barangays. The Municipal Accountant informed the Audit Team that the LGU has reconciled records and maintains records per taxpayer. The Municipal Accountant also stated that it is impractical to maintain ledger cards for each receivable but general ledger summary is maintained.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer is still working on encoding and providing the data to the Municipal Accountant; the Accounting Office considers it impractical to maintain detailed ledger cards though summary ledgers are maintained.

observations.recommendation: Require the contractor to expedite the completion of the project. The Municipality should have imposed liquidated damages for the delays. Also ensure stricter compliance with procurement and construction timelines in future projects.
observations.managementAction: The OIC Engineer confirmed that the new public market had been completed and is now operational. No liquidated damages was imposed to the contractor since they were able to finish the project within the approved extension period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality consider the full adoption of the guide in the creation and operation of its Local Economic Enterprises and to strengthen measures in view of their critical roles in the LGU development. We also recommended that the Honorable Members of the Sangguniang Bayan adopt an ordinance for the legal recognition of the said existing economic enterprises of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The LGU has been operating its economic enterprises and prepares separate financial reports. The MPDC stated that feasibility studies and documents are being prepared and will comply with DBM LBC No. 111 provisions.
observations.reason: Some requirements such as feasibility studies and specific DBM-required documents are not yet fully complied with; work in process.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor initiate the establishment of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board and the Multi-Purpose Environment Cooperatives or Association to promote the public participation; and ensure proper collection and transport mechanisms are in place.
observations.managementAction: As recognized the ongoing efforts of the Municipality to protect the public health and environment and in order to fully enjoy the benefits of Ecological Solid Waste Management, the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board has been created. The Management may opt to create an organization rather than a cooperative given the low participant interest.
observations.reason: Establishment of a cooperative may not be cost-effective given low participation; instead an organization was considered.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the GFPS Execom and TWG be fully constituted to strengthen the LGU GFPS; require the Municipal GFPS to complete its composition and ensure the GAD Database is established; integrate GAD activities in departmental plans and budgets and ensure monitoring and evaluation mechanisms are in place.
observations.managementAction: The GFPS Execom is now complete and fulfilling its tasks. The LGU is working on compiling the GAD database and aligning GAD plans with programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that disbursement vouchers and supporting documents are submitted to the Auditor in accordance with the cited laws and regulations to prevent issuance of audit suspension and/or disallowance and doubts on the regularity and propriety of the disbursements. Further, ensure accounts are kept in an organized manner and monitored for easy reporting.
observations.managementAction: Twenty six vouchers were submitted to the incumbent audit team and additional vouchers were submitted during the period of the incumbent audit team for verification. Some vouchers were missing but later submitted for audit.
observations.reason: Some disbursement vouchers remained unsubmitted at time of earlier audit but were later provided; search for remaining documents is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the GSO conduct an effective and reliable physical inventory
count of the PPE, taking into account only those actually existing and counted and
consider those items reported in lump sum or without particular details only for
reconciliation.

We also recommended that verification be made on the unrecorded PPE and
inexistent assets, particularly with regard to their ownership and existence,
respectively, prior to their recording in and dropping from the accounting books.
The Municipality may consider disposing unserviceable PPE to declutter the
accounting books of non-value items.

Lastly, we recommended that the Accounting Office and the GSO reconcile the
noted difference of P1,869,453.02 or disclose in the Notes to the Financial
Statements the results thereof as well as the amount of PPE items which had no
available documents or records.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to provide justification on the above-
discussed audit observation. They should strictly implement the guidelines set
forth in COA Circular No. 97-002 for the grant and liquidation of cash advance,
particularly with respect to the prescriptive period for the latter. Finally, the
Municipal Accountant must demand the submission of liquidation reports relative
to the P2,390,253.03 cash advances. Otherwise, they should be
considered unliquidated and the Municipal Accountant must withhold the salary of
the concerned employees and officials.

Foregoing considered, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires the
Municipal Treasurer to provide justification or explanation on the audit observation.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer Office (MTO) levies and collects
taxes and fees in accordance with the Municipality’s Revenue Code.

The MTO should also require the submission of sworn statement of gross
receipts/sales or certified copies of income tax return so as to come-up with an
accurate basis for the computation of business taxes, and to require the taxpayers
to duly accomplish the application form prior to processing.

Finally, the BPLO and the MTO should verify, assess and validate the noted
undercollection of P25,307.30 and consequently demand the collection/payment
thereof. The absence of documented action or information to counter the
undercollection shall mean the issuance of the corresponding Notice of Charge.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant withhold those
taxes arising from Accounts Payable still outstanding as of date and apply this
practice prospectively.

We recommended that the officials and employees concerned refrain from resorting to
procurement and disbursement through reimbursement basis and comply strictly the
relevant provisions of RA 9184. All payments should be made directly to the
supplier/contractor by check, as sound internal control prescribed under the imprest
system, and in order for the Municipality to deduct and withhold applicable taxes and remit
the same in accordance with the BIR regulations.

We also recommended that the Municipality design and implement consistent policy for
the payment of its communication expense, and which is aligned in the requirement of
imprest system.

Likewise, the planning and consolidation for the procurement of the agency must be
improved to minimize frequent but small purchases leading to payment through
reimbursement.

Considering that the vehicle rental is a necessity, the Management of the Municipality
must implement a formal and standard policy in its hiring, particularly with respect to the
selection of the vehicles, their rates, the execution of the contract, and others.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Officials responsible in approving payments
requires externally generated document to support the actual incurrence and correctness
of the transaction and to mitigate the risk of manipulation and fabrication of the same.

We recommended that the officials and employees concerned refrain from resorting
to procurement and disbursement through reimbursement basis and comply
strictly the relevant provisions of RA 9184 and BIR regulations on withholding
applicable taxes.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. the design and implementation of consistent policy for the payment of its
communication expense, and which is aligned in the requirement of imprest
system and on vehicle rentals, particularly with respect to the selection of
the vehicles, their rates, the execution of the contract, and others;
b. proper planning and consolidation for the procurement of the agency to
minimize frequent but small purchases of supplies
Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant require externally
generated document to support the actual incurrence and correctness of the
transaction and to mitigate the risk of manipulation and fabrication of the same.

Foregoing considered, we recommended that all official travels made by the
Municipal personnel be supported by properly accomplished Driver’s Trip Ticket
and that the Municipal Accountant must ensure the submission and completeness
of the same prior to her approval of payment for the fuel consumption.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer
account the P42,148.81 difference noted. Otherwise, refund of the same is
necessary.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Local Chief Executive to
coordinate with the PSOs in addressing the identified causes of delays and ensure
compliance with the IMA, procurement law, I-Build Operations Manual and other
pertinent rules and regulations in the subproject implementation.

We recommended that the Municipality impose liquidation damages, as well as
forfeit the contractor’s performance security and rescind the contract. We also
recommended that the Municipality formally terminate the contract in accordance
with the procedures set forth in Annex I of the 2016 Revised Rules of RA No. 9184.

The Municipality should also return to the BTr the unutilized ADM fund amounting to
P3,596,713.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Planning and Development Officer to submit the original and as-built
plans;
b. Municipal Accountant to compute and claim the liquidated damages for the
items delivered in delay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Budget Officer and MDRRM
Officer to provide justification on why expenses, the purpose thereof is not related to
disaster risk and response management activities, were charged to LDRRMF despite of
previous year’s audit observation. We also recommended that these kinds of
disbursements be paid out from the funds appropriated for the MDRRM Office under
the General Fund.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed and required the General
Services Officer include only those PPEs that are counted by the Inventory
Committee during the physical inventory taking in the RPCPPE and reconcile
results with the accounting records. Considering these findings on PPE as a
perennial issue of the Municipality, we recommended that these procedures be
done by account until all PPE accounts in the financial statements are completed.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to determine those
PPEs which had no available documents and disclose to the Notes to the
Financial Statements as well as the results of reconciliation of RPCPPE and
Inventory Report.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require
the Municipal Accountant to provide adequate disclosures on the cash inflows
and outflows from operating activities, particularly other receipts and other
payments that were not presented as among those items of which nature was
specifically classified in the face of the Statement of Cash Flows.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor require
the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to establish the certified list of taxpayers tallied
with the assessment report of the Municipal Assessor as of year-end;
b. establish the amount of real property taxes of those taxpayers with
legal issues for proper disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements
by the Municipal Accountant; and
c. Municipal Accountant maintain ledger cards for receivables of each
taxpayer with amounts reconciled with the records of the Municipal
Treasury.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the necessary
adjusting entries in the General Fund books. We also recommended and the
MPDC agreed, as required by the Municipal Mayor, to submit complete set of
documents to support the extended contract time granted to the contractor for
further evaluation.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed that:
• require the Accountant to maintain separate books of accounts taking all
its income and expenses since the time operations commenced; and
• require the cashier of the shop to use the official accountable form (AF 51)
in acknowledging payments for sales and other receipts of the shop and
remit all collections to the Treasurer for deposit to the municipality’s
authorized depository banks.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor agreed to refrain from granting time
extension for grounds that could have been determined in the detailed
engineering. Likewise, require the contractor to expedite the completion of the
project. We also recommended to duly impose liquidated damages on the 59 days
delay incurred.

Management’s comments:
The Municipal Mayor, Planning and Development personnel justified the need for the
major structural revisions. The Management will deliberate on whether to impose the
liquidated damages.
Auditor’s rejoinder:
The Audit Team does not question the necessity of the major structural revisions but
rather on the timing of revision considering that the contract is a design and build
scheme wherein the contractor should have determined at the outset, during detailed
engineering the necessary project base design to ensure a reliable structural integrity.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor agreed to submit to the Sangguniang
Bayan a request to institutionalize the BFAR approved CFLC operational plan.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the
necessary adjusting entries for the recording of CFLC building amounting to
₱2,999,661.19, donated equipment from BFAR and even the previously acquired
PPE funded by the trust fund in the General Fund book.

We strongly recommended and the Municipal Mayor required the General Services
Officer and the Inventory Committee to prioritize the conduct inventory and
inspection for the unserviceable properties and embody in an Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and facilitate their disposal of
in accordance with the aforecited rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a) Municipal Accountant:
• in coordination with the Municipal General Services Officer to determine
the details of the beginning balances of the Property, Plant and
Equipment (PPE) from their oldest available records for proper
accountability and adjustment of accounts;
• to evaluate the expenses charged and capitalized to the PPE accounts
despite being not directly attributable to them and effect appropriate
adjustments, if any, to arrive at the correct balances; and
• to recognize in the books the unrecorded assets arising from Trust Fund
for the current and prior periods.
b) Municipal General Services Officer to have an updated list of unserviceable
items for disposal and prepare all the necessary documents/reports to act on
the properties to be disposed within the reasonable time to prevent further
deterioration of the PPE.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the payees of the existence of the
stale checks for replacement, if applicable, and comply with the procedures in the
handling of stale/spoiled checks as stated in Section 59 of the NGAS Manual
Volume III; and
b) Municipal Accountant to effect necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct
balance of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency Current Account.
The suggested adjusting entry is as follow:
Cash in Bank – LCCA xxx
Accounts Payable xxx
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review and reconcile the noted
differences between the Bank Reconciliation Statements and the Cash in Bank
presented in the General Ledgers/Financial Statements. We further recommended
that the Municipal Accountant exert additional effort to regularly reconcile the
balances for the timely detection of variances, if any.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect in the books the necessary
adjustments to record all the reconciling items noted in this observation duly
supported with valid documents to reflect the correct cash balance in the financial
statements and coordinate with the respective depository bank for the reconciliation
of other reconciling items on the bank balance.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting
entries to take-up the interest income which is a reconciling item to reflect the correct
balance of the Cash in Bank – Local Currency Current account.
The suggested adjusting entries are as follow:
For prior year’s unrecorded interest income:
Cash in Bank – LCCA xxx
Government equity xxx
For current year’s unrecorded interest income:
Cash in Bank – LCCA xxx
Interest Income xxx

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a) Municipal Accountant
•••• reconcile the difference between the balances of Real Property Tax (RPT)
Receivable and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivable;
•••• conduct a regular reconciliation of records with the Municipal Treasurer
pertaining to the set-up RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred Income
Tax at the beginning of the period and the consistent adjustment of these
accounts upon collection;
•••• review all entries reflected in the general journal so as to ensure that all
recorded adjustments are correct and complete. Effect the necessary
adjustment/s, if any.
b) Municipal Treasurer
•••• conduct reconciliation of records with the Assessor’s Office in order to
have a uniform and updated taxpayers’ records for a more accurate
RPT/SET collectibles; and
•••• monitor payment of real property tax due for the current year and
strategize on collecting past due taxes.
c) the Municipal Mayor, as well as the Sangguniang Bayan Members, adopt
measures to collect from delinquent taxpayers and strengthen and intensify
collection system and tax campaign to minimize tax delinquencies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Municipal Accountant
• to record all purchases of supplies and materials to the inventory accounts
instead of treating the purchases as outright expenses instead of inventories,
except for fuel expenses and those purchases out of petty cash fund and all
issuances/utilization must be supported with RIS and SSMI and maintain
and regularly update the Subsidiary Ledger Cards (SLC) for each supplies
and materials;
• to recognize the Inventory account for purchases out of Trust Fund and
subsequently record the corresponding Expense account upon its
distribution to end-users to ensure proper accounting and promote better
control in the receipt and issuance of supplies and materials.
b) Municipal General Supply Officer
• to regularly maintain Stock Cards (SC) and Property Cards for supplies;
• to prepare and submit monthly the RSMI to the Municipal Accountant for
the recording of issuances; and
We further recommended that the Municipality create an Inventory team to conduct
physical inventory taking of all Inventory accounts each year and to submit the
reconciled RPCI together with the SC to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for
reconciliation with the SLC in order to ascertain the correct inventory balances for a
given period.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to continuously coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to
reconcile the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from/Due to LGUs by
identifying the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers for
adjustment, if warranted. Likewise, effect the immediate settlement of obligations of
each fund for a fair presentation of the cash position of each fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider the full adoption of the guide in
the creation and operation of its Local Economic Enterprises for the systematic
review of operations and adoption of appropriate measures in view of their critical
roles in the LGU development and their significant impact to good local
governance in accordance with DBM LBC No. 111 and the Manual on the Setting
Up and Operation of Local Economic Enterprises.
Likewise, we recommended that the Honorable Members of the Sanggunian Bayan
adopt an ordinance for the legal recognition of the said existing economic
enterprises of the Municipality.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Engineer, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and
other concerned officials to submit at the beginning of the year the lists of all on-
going government projects/programs/activities (“PPA”) and those that are to be
implemented during the year, in compliance with the above cited regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require/instruct:
a) the concerned municipal officials to submit the lacking documents to the
Auditor concerned for further evaluation and ensure completeness of the
bidding documents to establish propriety of the bidding process; and the
Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to refrain/stop the
processing of claims without complete documentations;
b) the BAC to observe the provisions of IRR of R.A. 9184 in the posting of notice of
award, notice to proceed and the approved contract in agency website. Moreover,
require the BAC Secretariat to issue certification on the posting of the
aforementioned documents in any conspicuous place in the premises of the
Procuring Entity;
c) the BAC to observe the procurement timelines provided under Annex C of RA
9184 and provide explanation for non-compliance on the period of action in
procurement activities;
d) the BAC to ensure accurateness of the submitted documents of the bidder
before declaring eligible;
e) the Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere to the provision of Section
17.4 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA 9184; and if a
treaty states otherwise, ensure that the reasons for the adoption of a different
rule are clearly reflected in the records of discussion;
f) the BAC Members, Secretariat and TWG to ensure that all of the eligibility
requirements submitted by the prospective bidder are in accordance with the
required form pursuant to R.A. 9184, its Revised IRR and GPPB issuances before
issuance of Notice of Post Qualification to the concerned contractor;
g) the BAC to strictly adhere with the pertinent provisions in Section 13 of IRR
of R.A. 9184 in inviting observers in all stages of the procurement process. Direct
them to indicate in the letter invitation with clarity the procurement stages to be
attended by the observers such as opening of bids, bid evaluation, pre-bid
conference, etc;
h) the Bids and Awards Committee to submit justification/explanation why the
project was awarded to the prospective bidder in spite absence of various
relevant eligibility documents.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor as Head of the Procuring Entity
and the contracting party adhere with the conditions set forth in the General and
Special Conditions of the Contract, Implementation Management Agreement
(IMA) and Operations Guidelines of Philippine Rural Development Project. Likewise,
the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer should be guided by various
provisions of PRDP guidelines particularly in making payment and refrain/stop
from paying transactions with incomplete supporting documents.
Further, the Head of the Procuring Entity should likewise give a written
warning/reminder to the contractor for not hitting the targeted accomplishment
within the scheduled timeframe.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned municipal
officials and employees to strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 3.1.1 of COA
Circular No. 2009-001 pertaining to the submission of contract/agreement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
a) the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to judiciously
plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Funds and to accelerate
their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality
regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management.
Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer to effect the immediate transfer of funds due
to Trust Fund for the unutilized LDRRMF for CY 2017.
b) the Municipal Accountant to exert due diligence to trace the specific unutilized
balances pertaining to CY 2011 and 2012 and subsequently revert the same to
the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund to be made available for other
social services after subsequent enactment by the Local Council. Henceforth,
stop the practice of charging expenses to the LDRRMF which has already
exceeded its five-year validity.
c) the Municipal Accountant to maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for transfers
of agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer to
facilitate the proper and systematic monitoring of the unutilized balances.
d) the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO)
to ensure that the future programs and activities charged to the 5% LDRRMF
are aligned to the Disaster Prevention and Preparedness goals of the
Municipality. Likewise, direct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate
funds for the MDRRM Office to avoid charging non-priority expenditures to
the calamity fund.
e) the Municipal Accountant disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the
amount and details of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF in order to inform
the users of relevant information.
f) the LDRRMO submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or
before the 15th day after the end of each month to the COA auditor pursuant to
COA Circular 2012-002 for the timely review and verification of the expenses
charged against the said fund.

We recommended that the Local School Board Council formulate additional
programs/projects/activities (PPAs) for health, nutrition, early education and social
services development related to the Early Childhood Care Development (ECCD)
Program that will directly address the basic holistic needs of young children and to
promote their optimum growth and development.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the Head of the Local School
Board, adhere strictly with the existing laws, rules and regulations in the
prioritization and utilization of Special Education Fund particularly for the operation
and maintenance of public schools, construction and repair of school buildings and
facilities to improve the delivery of basic education services.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) complete the composition of the GFPS Execom and TWG to ensure that the
LGU GFPS could fulfill its tasks and to strengthen the LGU GFPS.
b) establish the GAD Database to strengthen the planning and budgeting processes
for GAD and ensure that planned PPAs address identified gender issues, that
objectives, performance indicators and targets are well-formulated to facilitate
monitoring and evaluation for the attainment of GAD objectives.
c) mainstreaming of GAD related activities in all department/ offices of the
Municipality.
d) require the GAD Focal Point System and Technical Working Group to strictly
adhere to the reporting requirements of GAD, communicate any adjustments to
the GAD Plan and Budget through written communication to DILG in order to
properly monitor planned GAD programs/activities in accordance with the
approved budgets.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) instruct the GAD Focal Person to refrain from charging non gender-responsive
expenses against the fund allocated for Gender and Development; and
b) ensure the implementation of the approved GAD Plan and Budget in compliance
with the provisions of the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW,
DILG, DBM and NEDA in order to address more gender related issues and
contribute to the realization of the country’s commitment, plans and policies in
strengthening women empowerment, gender equality and gender development.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as the chairperson of the Local
Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC), direct the members of the Council
to prepare Local Development Plan for Children and Local Investment Plan for
Children, and maximize utilization of the allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs
provided for in DILG MC No 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 to ensure full
strengthening and implementation of the LCPC. Inform the Sanggunian Bayan
Members on the PPAs that can be funded by the 1% IRA for the strengthening of
LCPC.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant on the strict compliance of the previously cited auditing and accounting
laws and rules and regulations and the immediate submission of the listed
Disbursement Vouchers to the auditor to be subjected to audit and to prevent the
unnecessary issuance of audit suspension and/or disallowance.
Likewise, enforce the submission of the various Liquidation Reports together with the
corresponding supporting documents to the Audit Team to facilitate the post-audit of
the transactions to verify the regularity and propriety of the disbursements made.
Further, ensure that the accounts are kept in an organized and orderly manner and
properly monitored for easy reference of the agency or for other reporting purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the honorable members of
the Sanggunian include among its top priorities the construction of a building for
the safekeeping and accommodation of the increasing volume of documents
generated by the various departments/offices of the Municipality, particularly those
for submission to the audit team, with consideration on the preservation period of
ten (10) years as mandated by law.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee that will conduct a complete actual physical count of all LGU’s properties, prepare an inventory report thereon and submit the same to the Audit Team as required by the regulation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal General Services Officer relied upon the RPCPPE because although the GSO personnel asserted that they were able to conduct an actual count of the PPE as of the end of year, the report is still inclusive of the similar expenses and charges from prior periods which are not quantifiable as to physical existence.
observations.reason: The submitted RPCPPE could not be easily relied upon because although the GSO personnel asserted that they were able to conduct an actual count of the PPE as of the end of year, the report is still inclusive of similar expenses and charges from prior periods which are not quantifiable as to physical existence.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the perpetual inventory method in the accounting of supplies and materials; recognize the Inventory account for purchases out of Trust Fund and subsequently record the corresponding Expense account upon its distribution to end-users.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has submitted the justification to implement the perpetual inventory method in the accounting of supplies and materials and to recognize the Inventory account for purchases out of Trust Fund and subsequently record the corresponding expense upon distribution to end-users.
observations.reason: The Municipality still practiced the outright charging of various supplies expense accounts as of CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit monthly the Bank Reconciliation Statements within ten (10) days upon receipt at the end of each month in order to present a reliable balance of the Cash in Bank – LCCA account. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to submit the copies of Bank Reconciliation Statement and to strictly adhere with the rules on disclosing information in the notes to financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted the required copies of Bank Reconciliation Statements and the Schedule of Cash Advances. Also, the Municipality submitted the reconciliation of Net Cash Flows From Operating Activities and Reconciliation of Net Cash Flows From Operating Activities and Reconciliation of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant overlooked the interest income earned from the Savings Account being used for the eGOV Payment Facility, resulting to a minimal variance of ₱7.58 between the cashbooks and the general ledgers for the current year.
